whatThisIs: Reference six classic toxidromes with the signs that distinguish them, the most common offending agents, and the standard initial antidote or treatment: anticholinergic (hot, dry, red, mad - antihistamines, TCAs, scopolamine; physostigmine in select cases), cholinergic (SLUDGE, miosis, bradycardia - organophosphates, carbamates; atropine and pralidoxime), sympathomimetic (mydriasis, tachycardia, diaphoresis - cocaine, amphetamines; benzodiazepines), opioid (miosis, hypoventilation, somnolence - naloxone), sedative-hypnotic (somnolence, normal pupils - benzodiazepines and alcohols; flumazenil only in select cases), and serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia; cyproheptadine).
whenToUse: Use this at the bedside of any altered or hypothermic / hyperthermic patient where the differential includes intoxication, polypharmacy ingestion, or occupational exposure. Toxidrome recognition is the fastest way to narrow a broad differential because the syndromes are observation-based and do not require lab confirmation. Pair this reference with local Poison Control (1-800-222-1222 in the US) for dosing, repeat administration intervals, and case-specific guidance - the antidote is rarely the whole answer and decontamination, supportive care, and identification of co-ingestants drive outcomes.
